nativeRange.habitat: Seasonal floodplain pools, temporary ponds and marshes of the Argentine Pampas; ephemeral lowland water bodies that dry out completely during the austral summer dry season

decorPreferences: peat substrate or peat moss layer (minimum 5–10 cm depth) for egg deposition
dim lighting or floating plants to diffuse light
dense surface planting (floating plants such as water lettuce or frogbit)
dark substrate to reduce fish stress
minimal or no filtration to avoid disturbing substrate; air-powered sponge filter preferred

diet.notes: Obligate carnivore. Prefers live and frozen foods: live or frozen bloodworms, tubifex, daphnia, brine shrimp, and small aquatic invertebrates. Dry foods may be refused; high-quality micro-pellets or freeze-dried alternatives accepted by some specimens after conditioning. Feed small portions 1–2 times daily to avoid water fouling in small aquaria.

goodWith: small peaceful South American killifish of similar size

summary: Annual killifish from the Argentine Pampas; lifespan under 12 months. Adults bury eggs in peat that survive pond desiccation via diapause — eggs are stored dry and flooded to hatch fry. Cooler-tolerant than African Nothobranchius. Taxonomy is in flux: Costa (2002+) has split Cynolebias, moving many species to Austrolebias and allies.
careNotes: Cynolebias whitei is a true annual killifish endemic to the seasonal pools of the Argentine Pampas. Its entire wild life cycle completes in under 12 months: adults spawn into the muddy substrate of ephemeral pools, which dry out completely each year; the buried eggs survive desiccation via embryonic diapause and hatch only when rains return. In the aquarium this annual biology is reproduced by housing a trio (1 male, 2 females) in a small species-only tank with a deep layer of peat moss or compressed peat as a spawning medium. Eggs are collected every 4–6 weeks by siphoning or removing the peat, which is then gently squeezed and stored slightly moist in a sealed plastic bag at 18–22°C for 10–16 weeks before being flooded with soft, slightly acidic water to trigger hatching. Water temperature should be kept cool (16–24°C) reflecting the temperate Argentine climate; unlike African Nothobranchius, C. whitei tolerates and benefits from temperatures well below 24°C. Water quality must be maintained carefully: peat filtration or blackwater extract helps simulate the tannin-rich, soft, mildly acidic native water. Males can be mildly territorial during spawning but rarely cause serious injury; females may be kept 2:1 with the male to distribute attention. The fish are short-lived even under optimal conditions; hobbyists should expect adults to decline visibly at 8–12 months of age regardless of husbandry. Taxonomic note: the genus Cynolebias has been progressively revised since Costa (2002), with numerous former Cynolebias species reassigned to Austrolebias (the most species-rich split genus), Megalebias, Simpsonichthys, and others. C. whitei may be subject to further reclassification; verify current nomenclature against FishBase or Catalog of Fishes before purchase or publication.
breedingNotes: Egg-burying annual killifish. Condition a breeding pair on live or frozen foods for 1–2 weeks before introducing to a peat-lined spawning tank. Spawning occurs by the pair descending together into the peat substrate, releasing and fertilising eggs 1–5 at a time; this substrate-diving behaviour is characteristic of Cynolebias and related annual genera. Collect peat every 4–6 weeks, squeeze excess water until barely moist, seal in a labelled zip-lock bag, and store in the dark at 18–22°C. After 10–16 weeks of diapause, flood the peat with 2–3 cm of soft, slightly warm water (20–22°C); most fry hatch within 24–48 hours. Feed fry micro-foods immediately: infusoria, microworms, and newly hatched brine shrimp nauplii. Fry grow rapidly and reach sexual maturity within 6–8 weeks. Multiple diapause stages (DI, DII, DIII) have been described in annual killifish eggs; incubation duration may need adjustment if development is slow. Brood sizes are typically 20–60 eggs per collection.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Cynolebias whitei
primary.notes: Primary source for adult size (males to 6–7 cm), temperature range, pH, hardness, native range (Argentina, Entre Ríos province), habitat description, spawning behaviour, diapause egg care protocol, and diet. Also confirms family Rivulidae, order Cyprinodontiformes, and notes taxonomic uncertainty within the Cynolebias/Austrolebias complex.

additional.name: FishBase — Cynolebias whitei
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Rivulidae, Cyprinodontiformes), adult size (up to 7 cm TL), native range (La Plata basin, Argentina), and temperature tolerance. Confirms the species is listed under Cynolebias but notes synonymy/reclassification discussions linked to Austrolebias-group revisions by Costa 2002.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Killifish Care Guide
additional.notes: General annual killifish husbandry guide. Used to cross-check peat spawning protocol, egg diapause storage conditions, fry feeding recommendations, and carnivore diet guidance applicable to South American annual killifish including Cynolebias.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Annual killifish and diapause eggs
additional.notes: Trade-press feature covering annual killifish breeding methodology including peat storage, diapause phases (DI/DII/DIII), and hatching triggers. Supplemented egg collection and storage protocol in breedingNotes.

additional.name: Catalog of Fishes (Eschmeyer) via FishBase — Cynolebias
additional.notes: Nomenclatural reference confirming authorship (Myers, 1942) and current genus placement. Documents synonymy history relevant to the Cynolebias/Austrolebias taxonomic split and the ongoing revision of South American annual killifish genera.

fish.breedingNotes: Peat-substrate egg burier; eggs require 10–16 weeks dry diapause storage before flooding to trigger hatching. Fry are small and require live micro-foods. Accessible to killifish hobbyists but requires understanding of annual killifish diapause management.
